1: Wail, ships of Tarshish — for Tyre is destroyed! The oracle against Tyre, the great Phoenician commercial empire, opens Isaiah's sequence of judgments on the nations. The wailing Tarshish ships mourning the fall of their commercial anchor directly prefigure Revelation 18:17-19: every sea captain, every passenger, every sailor... stood far off and cried out... Was there ever a city like this great city? John's Babylon-mourning draws explicitly on this Tyre-oracle, and both reach their resolution in Christ's final triumph over the commercial-political systems that substitute wealth for covenant.
2: Be still, you people of the coastland — you whom Sidon's merchants made wealthy. The silence commanded of the coastlands before Tyre's judgment is the awe-struck stillness before divine action. Be still and know that I am God (Psalms 46:10) — the same stillness that follows Christ's rebuke of the sea (Mark 4:39: Peace! Be still!). The silence before YHWH's judgment on commercial empire prefigures the silence in heaven before the Lamb breaks the seventh seal (Revelation 8:1) — the hush before the eschatological consummation.
3: The grain of Shihor — the Nile's harvest — crossed the great waters and became her income; she was the market town of nations. Tyre as the international grain-market that fed the known world makes her the commercial center of ancient civilization. Jesus as the true bread of life (John 6:35: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never hunger) replaces the commercial grain-market with himself. The market town of nations that distributed Egypt's grain is transcended by the one through whom all true nourishment flows.
4: Be ashamed, O Sidon — the sea's own fortress declares: I have not travailed or given birth; I have raised no young men. The barren sea-city shamed by her inability to produce life. The barren powers that cannot generate covenant community contrast with the birth announced in Isaiah 7:14 and Isaiah 9:6 — the virgin who conceives, the child who is born, the one through whom life enters what could not produce it. The sea's boast of barrenness meets its answer in the incarnation: God does what the powers cannot, taking the form of the son the sea could not birth.
5: When the news reaches Egypt, they will writhe with anguish at what has happened to Tyre. International grief at the commercial empire's collapse mirrors Revelation 18:9-10: the kings of the earth who committed adultery with her and shared her luxury will weep and mourn over her. The concentric circles of grief — Tyre's trade partners lamenting her fall — are the NT pattern for the judgment on Babylon, which Christ's return executes. The mourning is genuine but futile; no one can reverse what YHWH has decreed.
6: Sail across to Tarshish — wail, you people of the coastland! Tarshish, the western extremity of the trading world, is the destination of desperate flight. Jonah's attempt to flee YHWH's commission by taking ship to Tarshish (Jonah 1:3) is the individual acting out of the same reflex — the commercial escape route from covenant obligation. Jesus cites the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:39-41) as the definitive sign for his generation: three days in the fish, three days in the tomb. The Tarshish-ship that swallowed Jonah is the same commercial-maritime world that cannot hide from YHWH's purposes.
7: Is this the city so ancient in its origins — whose people sailed out to settle far-off shores? Tyre's founding-city status (she planted Carthage and other colonies across the Mediterranean) made her the originating power of the western commercial world. Against all claims of human antiquity and civilizational precedence, Jesus places his own: before Abraham was, I am (John 8:58). The city whose origins are ancient (miqedem) is nonetheless subject to the one who exists before all origins — the eternal I AM who entered human time as the Messiah.
8: Who planned this against Tyre — the crown-bestowing city, whose traders were princes? The merchant-princes of Tyre represent the fusion of commercial and political power that constitutes worldly empire. Jesus's teaching confronts this fusion directly: it is easier for a camel to pass through a needle's eye than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God (Matthew 19:24). The crown-bestowing city gives crowns to its commercial elite; the Lamb gives his followers a crown of life (Revelation 2:10) purchased by an entirely different economy.
9: The LORD of hosts planned it — to strip the pride from all magnificence and to humiliate all the famous of the earth. YHWH's explicit purpose in Tyre's fall is the comprehensive humiliation of human magnificence. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:51-52) announces the identical program as the inaugurating act of Christ's coming: he has scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts; he has brought down the mighty from their thrones. The incarnation itself — God entering the world as a helpless infant — is the enactment of what this verse prophesies: pride stripped, magnificence humiliated.
10: Flow through your land like the Nile, O daughter of Tarshish — nothing to hold you back any longer. The release of the commercial flow after Tyre's collapse — nothing restraining it — uses the Nile-flood image of abundance. Jesus's promise in John 7:38-39 reverses the commercial imagery: from his innermost being rivers of living water will flow — and this he said about the Spirit. Where commercial rivers of grain and gold flowed through Tyre, the Spirit-river now flows from those who believe. The unrestrainable flow of commerce becomes the image for the unrestrainable flow of the Spirit.
11: YHWH stretched his hand over the sea and shook the kingdoms — he issued his order against Phoenicia to demolish its fortresses. The hand stretched over the sea repurposes the Exodus gesture (Exodus 14:21: Moses stretched his hand and the sea parted) for commercial-empire judgment. The same divine authority over the waters that rescued Israel from Egypt now dismantles the commercial fortresses of Phoenicia. Jesus's calming of the storm on Galilee (Mark 4:39) exercises this identical sovereignty — the creator's authority over the sea that was always his to command.
12: Your days of celebration are finished, O crushed virgin daughter of Sidon — even in Cyprus you will have no rest. The exile who finds no rest even in the refuge city — the perpetual restlessness of the displaced. Jesus's invitation (Matthew 11:28-30: Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest) is the answer to exactly this condition. The Sidonian exile fleeing from city to city with no rest available finds in Christ the rest that no commercial-maritime refuge can provide. The yoke of empire is exchanged for the easy yoke of the Messiah.
13: Look at the land of the Chaldeans — a people who were nothing until Assyria raised them up. The Chaldeans (Babylonians) who destroyed Tyre were themselves a people of no account until YHWH used Assyria to elevate them as his instrument. The pattern of YHWH raising the nobodies to execute his purposes against the somebodies runs through the NT: God chose the foolish to shame the wise, the weak to shame the strong, the despised and the things that are not, to bring to nothing the things that are (1 Corinthians 1:27-28). The Chaldeans-from-nothing are the prototype of gospel reversal.
14: Wail, ships of Tarshish — your fortress harbour is destroyed! The second wailing of the Tarshish fleet brackets the oracle. Revelation 18:19 echoes this directly: they threw dust on their heads and cried out, weeping and mourning: Was there ever a city like this great city? The merchant ships mourning their commercial anchor is the pre-figuration of the final mourning over Babylon-the-great, which Christ's return brings to its conclusion in Revelation 18-19's sequence of judgment and triumph.
15: Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years — the span of one king's lifetime. The seventy years of Tyre's oblivion runs parallel to the seventy years of Babylon's dominance over Israel (Jeremiah 25:11-12; Daniel 9:2). Daniel's meditation on these seventy years leads Gabriel to extend the framework to seventy weeks of years (Daniel 9:24-27) — the prophetic timeline within which NT scholarship has understood the Messianic fulfillment. The seventy-year exile-and-restoration template is the chronological frame through which Christ's coming is interpreted.
16: Pick up your harp, walk the streets, O forgotten prostitute — play your sweetest tune so someone might remember you. The commercial city reasserting herself through entertainment and seduction uses the harp as the instrument of self-advertisement. Revelation 18:22 announces the end of exactly this music: the sound of harpists and musicians, of flute players and trumpeters, will never be heard in you again. The harp music that sustains Babylon's self-promotional seduction is silenced by Christ's return, replaced by the new song of the redeemed (Revelation 14:2-3: the sound of harpists playing their harps before the Lamb — the redemption of music itself).
17: At the end of seventy years YHWH will attend to Tyre and she will return to her trading — selling herself to all the kingdoms of the world. Tyre's restoration involves a return to the same prostitute-commerce with all the kingdoms. This is precisely Revelation's description of Babylon: all the nations have drunk the maddening wine of her adulteries... the kings of the earth committed adultery with her (Revelation 17:2; Isaiah 18:3). The commercial-political compromise that Isaiah names as prostitution is the defining feature of every earthly system that substitutes the kingdoms-of-the-world for the kingdom of God — the same temptation Satan offered Jesus (Matthew 4:8-9).
18: Her merchandise and earnings will be set apart as holy to the LORD — not stored or hoarded; her profit will supply those who live before the LORD with abundant food and fine clothing. The oracle's redemptive conclusion: even Tyre's commercial wealth will ultimately be consecrated to YHWH. The wealth of nations flowing into the covenant community rather than being accumulated by commercial empire anticipates Revelation 21:24-26: the kings of the earth will bring their splendor into it [the New Jerusalem]... the glory and honor of the nations will be brought into it. The final transformation of commercial wealth into holy provision is the eschatological resolution of the entire Tyre-oracle: not destruction but conversion, under Christ's sovereign rule.
